Mechatronics final
  • API Reference
  • View page source

API Reference

main

Top-level robot setup and scheduler startup for the Mechatronics project.

This module wires together the hardware drivers, shared variables, queues, and cooperative tasks used by the Romi robot. When run on the microcontroller, it creates all runtime objects and starts the cooperative scheduler. When imported by Sphinx, the runtime setup is skipped so the module can be documented without starting the robot.

main.main()[source]

Instantiate robot components and run the cooperative scheduler.

main.print_ram_usage(label='RAM')[source]

Print current heap usage (for debugging MemoryError).

motor

class motor.Motor(PWM, DIR, nSLP, timernumber, channelnumber)[source]

A motor driver interface encapsulated in a Python class. Works with motor drivers using separate PWM and direction inputs such as the DRV8838 drivers present on the Romi chassis from Pololu.

disable()[source]

Disables the motor driver by taking it into sleep mode

enable()[source]

Enables the motor driver by taking it out of sleep mode into brake mode

set_effort(effort)[source]

Sets the effort requested from the motor based on an input value between -100 and 100.

param effort:

The effort requested from the motor.

type effort:

int

returns:

None

encoder

Quadrature encoder interface used by the Romi drivetrain.

class encoder.Encoder(tim, chA_pin, chA, chB_pin, chB, direction=1)[source]

A quadrature encoder decoding interface encapsulated in a Python class

get_position()[source]

Returns the most recently updated value of position as determined within the update() method :param: None :returns: The most recently updated value of position as determined within the update() method

get_rpm()[source]

Return the most recently computed shaft speed in RPM.

Returns:

Motor speed in revolutions per minute.

Return type:

float

get_velocity()[source]

Return the most recently computed encoder velocity.

Returns:

Velocity in counts per microsecond.

Return type:

float

update()[source]

Updates the encoder position and velocity. param: None Returns: None Notes: Delta = change in timer counter only (16-bit wrap). position += delta.

zero()[source]

Sets the present encoder position to zero and causes future updates to measure with respect to the new zero.

Param:

None

Returns:

None

encoder.ticks_diff(new, old)

Provide a MicroPython-like fallback for desktop doc builds.

encoder.ticks_us()

Provide a MicroPython-like fallback for desktop doc builds.

IMU driver

class IMU_driver.BNO055(i2c, address)[source]
cal_status()[source]

Return a human-readable calibration summary string.

The returned string reports the calibration state of the overall system, gyroscope, accelerometer, and magnetometer.

Returns:

Multi-line calibration status report.

Return type:

str

euler()[source]

Returns the Euler angles of the IMU.

Param:

None

Returns:

angles (The Euler angles of the IMU)

Return type:

tuple

Notes:

tuple returned - (heading,roll,pitch)

get_accel_mps2()[source]

Returns the unit-converted acceleration of the IMU.

Param:

None

Returns:

lin_accels (The linear acceleration of the IMU)

Return type:

tuple

Notes:

tuple returned - (accX,accY,accZ) each an integer in m/s^2. Due to orientation of the IMU on the robot X and Y are switched.

get_accel_raw()[source]

Returns the raw acceleration of the IMU. :param: None :returns: accels (The raw acceleration of the IMU) :return type: tuple :notes: tuple returned - (accX,accY,accZ) each 16bit signed int raw register value.

get_ang_velocity()[source]

Returns the angular velocity of the IMU. :param: None :returns: omegas (The angular velocity of the IMU) :return type: tuple :notes: tuple returned - (gyrX,gyrY,gyrZ)

get_linear_accel_mps2()[source]

Returns the unit-converted linear acceleration of the IMU. :param: None :returns: lin_accels (The linear acceleration of the IMU) :return type: tuple :notes: tuple returned - (linx,liny,linz) each an integer in m/s^2. Due to orientation of the IMU on the robot X and Y are switched.

get_linear_accel_raw()[source]

Returns the linear acceleration of the IMU (gravity compensated by fusion).

Param:

None

Returns:

lin_accels (The linear acceleration of the IMU)

Return type:

tuple

Notes:

tuple returned - (linX,linY,linZ) each 16bit signed int raw value.

get_mode()[source]

Returns the current mode of the IMU. :param: None :returns: mode (The current mode of the IMU) :return type: str

heading()[source]

Returns the heading of the IMU. :param: None :returns: heading (The heading of the IMU) :return type: int :notes: heading is in degrees.

read_cal_coeffs()[source]

reads the calibration coefficents of the IMU and puts them into a tuple that has order accX,accY,accZ,magX,magY,magZ,gyoX,gyoY,gyoZ,accR,magR

Param:

None None

Returns:

values (The calibration coefficients of the IMU)

Return type:

tuple

Notes:

tuple returned - (accX,accY,accZ,magX,magY,magZ,gyoX,gyoY,gyoZ,accR,magR)

set_mode(mode)[source]

Takes in a string and sets the mode of the IMU to one of the 5 fusion modes

Param:

mode (The mode to set the IMU to)

Parameters:

type – str

Raises:

ValueError: If mode is not a valid fusion mode on IMU (CONFIG, IMU, COMPASS, M4G, NDOF, NDOFoff).

Returns:

None

write_cal_coeffs(blob)[source]

writes calibration coefficents into the imu :param: blob (The calibration coefficients of the IMU) :param type: tuple :returns: None :notes: tuple returned - (accX,accY,accZ,magX,magY,magZ,gyoX,gyoY,gyoZ,accR,magR)

yaw_rate()[source]

Returns the yaw rate of the IMU. :param: None :returns: yaw (The yaw rate of the IMU) :return type: int :notes: yaw is in degrees/second.

Task motor

Motor-task state machine for encoder sampling and motor actuation.

class task_motor.task_motor(mot: Motor, enc: Encoder, start, startTimeRef, line, pos, err, go, dataValues, timeValues)[source]

Task that updates one motor/encoder pair within the scheduler.

run()[source]

Run one iteration of the motor task state machine.

Yields:

int – The current motor-task state identifier.

Task user

Serial user-interface task for tuning, test runs, and data logging.

class task_user.task_user(setpoint, gainP, gainI, speed, line, leftMotorGo, rightMotorGo, leftDataValues, leftTimeValues, rightDataValues, rightTimeValues, centroidValues, centroidTime, estx, esty, startTime=None)[source]

User-interface task for serial control of the robot and test flows.

run()[source]

Run one iteration of the user-interface state machine.

Yields:

int – The current UI state identifier.

Task controller

Closed-loop controller and game-state logic for the Romi robot.

This task contains the position controller used for single-motor testing, the line-following controller, and the state-estimation/game-track logic used during the final autonomous run.

class task_controller.task_controller(Kp, Ki, setpoint, speed, start, line, R_pos, R_err, L_pos, L_err, centroidValues, centroidTime, current_x, current_y, heading, dist, accl)[source]

Controller task for setpoint, line-following, and track navigation.

run()[source]

Run one iteration of the controller state machine.

Yields:

int – The current controller state identifier.

Task observer

State observer for robot pose estimation using encoders and the IMU.

class task_observer.task_observer(IMU, R_pos, R_err, L_pos, L_err, estx, esty, start, current_x, current_y, heading, dist, accl)[source]

Observer task that estimates robot position and heading.

run()[source]

Run one iteration of the observer state machine.

Yields:

int – The current observer state identifier.

Thread-safe queues and shared variables for cooperative tasks.

This module provides queue and share abstractions that allow tasks to pass data without risk of corruption from interrupts or pre-emptive multithreading. It is designed for MicroPython-based embedded systems using the pyb API.

Typical usage:

Using a queue:

import task_share

# This queue holds unsigned short (16-bit) integers
my_queue = task_share.Queue('H', 100, name='My Queue')

# In one task, put data into the queue
my_queue.put(some_data)

# In another task, read data from the queue
something = my_queue.get()

Using a share:

import task_share

# This share holds a signed short (16-bit) integer
my_share = task_share.Share('h', name='My Share')

# In one task, write data
my_share.put(some_data)

# In another task, read data
something = my_share.get()
Module contents:

  • BaseShare – Common base class for Queue and Share.

  • Queue – Ring buffer for passing multiple values between tasks.

  • Share – Single-value container for sharing the latest value.

  • share_list – Global list of all shares and queues (for diagnostics).

  • type_code_strings – Mapping from array type codes to readable type names.

  • show_all() – Return a diagnostic string describing all shares/queues.

Copyright (c) 2017-2023 JR Ridgely. Released under the GNU Public License v3.0.

class task_share.BaseShare(type_code, thread_protect=True, name=None)[source]

Base class for queues and shares which exchange data between tasks.

This class isn’t used directly; it just factors out behavior common to Queue and Share (type code, thread protection, and registration in share_list).

class task_share.Queue(type_code, size, thread_protect=False, overwrite=False, name=None)[source]

Queue used to transfer data from one task to another.

A Queue is a ring buffer holding elements of a uniform type (given by an array type code). It is safe for use with interrupts or pre-emptive scheduling if thread_protect is enabled.

Example:

import task_share

# Unsigned 16-bit samples
samples = task_share.Queue('H', 100, name='Samples')

# In producer task
samples.put(value)

# In consumer task
if samples.any():
    v = samples.get()
any()

Return True if there is at least one item in the queue.

clear()[source]

Remove all contents from the queue and reset counters.

empty()

Return True if the queue is empty.

full()

Return True if the queue is full (no more room for new items).

get(in_ISR=False)

Read and remove one item from the queue.

This method blocks (busy-waits) until an item is available.

Parameters:

in_ISR – True if called from interrupt context.

Returns:

The dequeued item.

num_in()

Return the current number of items in the queue.

put(item, in_ISR=False)

Put an item into the queue.

If the queue is full and overwrite is False, this method blocks (busy-waits) until there is room. In an ISR, it returns immediately if the queue is full.

Parameters:

  • item – Value to enqueue.

  • in_ISR – True if called from an interrupt context.

ser_num = 0
class task_share.Share(type_code, thread_protect=True, name=None)[source]

Single-value shared data item used between tasks.

A Share holds the latest value of a single variable of a given type. With thread_protect enabled, reads and writes are protected against corruption by interrupts or pre-emptive scheduling.

Example:

import task_share

my_share = task_share.Share('h', name='My Share')
my_share.put(value)
latest = my_share.get()
get(in_ISR=False)

Read and return the current value of the share.

Parameters:

in_ISR – True if called from interrupt context.

Returns:

The value currently stored in the share.

put(data, in_ISR=False)

Write a new value into the share, overwriting any previous value.

Parameters:

  • data – New value to store.

  • in_ISR – True if called from interrupt context.

ser_num = 0
task_share.show_all()[source]

Return a diagnostic string describing all queues and shares.

Returns:

One line per share/queue, joined with newlines.

Return type:

str

Cooperative task scheduling for MicroPython multitasking.

This module provides classes to run cooperatively scheduled tasks in a multitasking system. Tasks are created as generators: functions with infinite loops that call yield at least once per loop. References to all tasks are kept in a list maintained by TaskList; the scheduler runs each task’s generator according to a chosen algorithm (e.g. round-robin or highest-priority-first).

Example

Create a task and run it with the scheduler:

def task1_fun():
    '''Switch states repeatedly.'''
    state = 0
    while True:
        if state == 0:
            state = 1
        elif state == 1:
            state = 0
        yield state

task1 = cotask.Task(task1_fun, name='Task 1', priority=1,
                    period=500, profile=True, trace=True)
cotask.task_list.append(task1)
while True:
    cotask.task_list.pri_sched()
Module contents:

  • Task – Cooperative task with optional profiling and tracing.

  • TaskList – Container of tasks; supports round-robin and priority scheduling.

  • SCHEDULER_DEBUG_MAX – Maximum number of scheduler debug entries to keep (default 64; oldest dropped when full).

  • task_list – Default TaskList instance created on import; append tasks here and call TaskList.pri_sched() or TaskList.rr_sched() to run them.

Copyright (c) 2017-2023 JR Ridgely. Released under the GNU Public License v3.0. Intended for educational use; use is not limited thereto.

class cotask.Task(run_fun, name='NoName', priority=0, period=None, profile=False, trace=False, shares=())[source]

Cooperative task with scheduling and optional performance logging.

Implements behavior common to tasks in a cooperative multitasking system under MicroPython. A task can be scheduled by time (period) or by an external trigger (e.g. interrupt or another task). State transitions can be traced and run times profiled. Task code must be a generator that yields the current state (and the CPU) after running for a short, bounded time.

get_trace()[source]

Return a string with the task’s state transition trace.

The trace lists (time, from_state, to_state) for each transition, if tracing was enabled. Can be large.

Returns:

String showing state transitions, or “not traced” if tracing was disabled.

go()[source]

Mark the task as ready to run.

Can be called from an interrupt service routine or from another task when data is available for this task to process.

ready() → bool

Return whether the task is ready to run.

For timer-based tasks, checks if the next run time has been reached. For event-triggered tasks, checks the go flag. Can be overridden in subclasses for custom readiness logic.

Returns:

True if the task is ready to run, False otherwise.

reset_profile()[source]

Reset execution-time profiling counters.

Also used internally by __init__() to initialize profiling variables.

schedule() → bool[source]

Run this task once if it is ready (called by the scheduler).

If the task is not ready, returns immediately. If ready, runs the task’s generator up to the next yield() and then returns.

Returns:

True if the task ran, False if it did not.

set_period(new_period)[source]

Set the period between task runs.

Parameters:

new_period – New period in milliseconds, or None to make the task event-triggered (via go()) instead of time-based.

class cotask.TaskList[source]

List of tasks used by the scheduler.

Holds all tasks that will be run by the scheduler. Typically you only add tasks (append) and call the scheduler (rr_sched or pri_sched). The list is sorted by priority so the scheduler can quickly find the highest-priority ready task. Scheduling can be priority-based or round-robin.

append(task)[source]

Append a task to the list.

The list is kept sorted by priority (highest first) so the scheduler can quickly find the highest-priority ready task.

Parameters:

task – Task instance to add (e.g. a Task).

pri_sched()

Run the single highest-priority task that is ready.

Each call finds the highest-priority task that is ready and runs it once (one generator step). Within a priority level, tasks are scheduled in round-robin order.

rr_sched()

Run tasks in round-robin order, ignoring priority.

Each call iterates through all tasks and gives each one a chance to run. Higher-priority tasks are visited first, but over time every task runs about equally often.

Created on Wed Feb 25 18:45:39 2026

@author: maxwe

class linesen.sensor(pin1, pin2, pin3, pin4, pin5)[source]

creates a class that can get the current line position in terms of a 5 IR sensor array

get_pos()[source]

returns the current position of the line sensor, where zero position is equivilient to centered, :param: None :returns: pos (The current position of the line sensor) :return type: float :notes: float returned - the position of the line sensor, where zero position is equivilient to centered, pointing left -> positive, pointing right -> negative returns for 100 no read
